topicArea: Literary Realism, the Reality Effect & Plausibility as Convention

questions.passage:
The reviewer's highest praise arrives in a familiar formula: the novel is so real that she forgot she was reading. The compliment deserves a moment of bafflement. Every person in the book was invented, every conversation composed, every rainstorm scheduled by a writer at a desk; the book is artifice from first word to last. What, then, is being praised when a made thing is praised for seeming unmade? The question sounds pedantic and turns out to be the door to the whole problem of realism, a term that literary culture uses constantly and can rarely define without contradiction.

The intuitive answer holds that realism is correspondence: the realist novel depicts life as it actually is, ordinary people, plausible events, recognizable settings, and its accuracy is what the reviewer salutes. The answer collapses quickly. Life as it actually is has no chapters, no protagonists, no proportion between prominence and importance; it does not end its episodes at meaningful junctures or distribute attention according to significance. Every narrative selects, compresses, arranges, and orders, and the ordering is precisely what makes it narrative rather than surveillance. A novel that truly corresponded to life would be as unreadable as an unedited transcript of it, which no one calls art and no one finishes.

A sturdier account holds that realism is not correspondence but convention, a repertoire of techniques that produce the effect of the real. The repertoire is identifiable: protagonists of middling station rather than princes; circumstantial detail rendered with inventory precision; motivation traced to intelligible causes; narration that slips inside minds and reports their weather; and a tactful concealment of the machinery, no addresses to the reader, no visible authorial hand arranging the furniture. None of these is reality; each is a device for signifying it, and the proof is historical. Techniques that struck one generation as transparency itself strike later generations as mannerism, visible, dated, quaint, which could not happen if the techniques were windows rather than styles. Realism, on this account, is the set of artifices a given audience has stopped noticing.

One device merits its own paragraph, because its logic is so instructive. Realist prose abounds in details that serve no narrative purpose, the barometer on the parlor wall, the precise contents of a sideboard, objects that advance no plot and reveal no character. Their function, one influential analysis proposed, is exactly their uselessness: by resisting assimilation to the story's design, the pointless detail declares that this world exists beyond the tale's requirements, and thereby certifies the whole. Meaning is sacrificed to produce the signature of the real. The device rewards attention because it shows realism operating not by resembling reality but by strategically performing reality's indifference to storytelling.

Against the realist repertoire stands a counter-tradition with a moral charge. Realism, these writers argue, is not innocent craft but quiet deception: its tidy causality, legible motives, and concealed machinery train readers to expect a world more explicable than the one they inhabit, and its very smoothness is an ideology of coherence. Honesty, on this view, requires showing the seams, fragmentation, interruption, the author stepping visibly into the frame, so that the reader is never allowed to forget that a made thing is being made. The polemic lands a genuine blow, and then, with time, suffers a familiar fate: the seams become a style, the interruptions become expected, readers acclimate, and the devices of exposure settle into a repertoire of their own, as conventional as the concealments they indicted. There is no form so disruptive that habit cannot domesticate it.

What survives these rounds is a humbler and more interesting conception. The praise so real never asserted correspondence; it reported an experience, the experience of encountering represented life that matches the reader's sense of how life goes. That sense is not private; it is a socially maintained expectation, and plausibility is best understood as a treaty, perpetually renegotiated, between writers and readers about which artifices currently count as none. The treaty explains what correspondence cannot: why realism has a history, why it varies across audiences, and why a novel can feel truer than the documentary record, since invention, freed from what happened, can be calibrated entirely to what convinces.

The reviewer, then, was not deceived, and her formula needs only translation. The novel did not escape artifice; it achieved the artifice her moment does not register as artifice, honoring the current treaty so exactly that its craft passed as absence of craft. That is a real achievement and a perishable one. The treaty will be renegotiated, the invisible devices will become visible, and some future reader, encountering the same pages, will see, where the reviewer saw life itself, the unmistakable handwriting of a period style.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Realism is neither correspondence to life nor innocent craft, but a historically shifting treaty between writers and readers about which artifices currently pass unnoticed, so the praise so real reports a convention working perfectly rather than artifice escaped.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The realist novel depicts life as it actually is, which is why readers correctly praise its accuracy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anti-realist counter-tradition succeeded in permanently exposing realism's deceptions, rendering its techniques unusable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reviewers who claim to forget they are reading have been deceived by novelists into mistaking fiction for documentary fact.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage dismantles the correspondence account, life has no chapters, unedited transcript, builds the convention account through the repertoire and the useless detail, shows the anti-realist polemic domesticated into a repertoire of its own, and lands on plausibility as a perpetually renegotiated treaty, concluding that the reviewer's novel achieved the artifice her moment does not register as artifice, a real and perishable achievement.
explanations.B: This choice restates the intuitive answer the second paragraph collapses. Life as it actually is lacks protagonists, chapters, and proportion, every narrative selects and arranges, and true correspondence would be unreadable, so accuracy in this sense cannot be what the praise correctly salutes.
explanations.C: This choice grants the counter-tradition a permanence the fifth paragraph denies. The polemic lands a genuine blow and then suffers the familiar fate: seams become style, readers acclimate, and the devices of exposure settle into conventions of their own, so no permanent unmasking occurred and realist techniques remain in use under the current treaty.
explanations.D: This choice contradicts the final paragraph's explicit verdict. The reviewer was not deceived; her formula reports an experience produced by craft honoring the treaty so exactly that it passes as absence of craft, which the passage calls a real achievement rather than a successful trick on a confused reader.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: list the complete repertoire of realist techniques introduced in the previous paragraph.

choices.label: B
choices.text: concede that some details in realist fiction genuinely correspond to real objects.

choices.label: C
choices.text: examine a single instructive device, the narratively useless detail, whose pointlessness certifies the fictional world by performing reality's indifference to storytelling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: argue that novelists should remove details that serve no narrative purpose.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes a magnification for a catalog. The repertoire, middling protagonists, circumstantial detail, concealed machinery, is the third paragraph's inventory; the fourth explicitly isolates one device that merits its own paragraph because its logic is so instructive.
explanations.B: This choice smuggles the correspondence account back in through the sideboard. The paragraph's analysis is anti-correspondence at its core: the detail works not by matching a real object but by its uselessness within the design, signifying the real rather than resembling it.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph takes the barometer and the sideboard contents, details advancing no plot and revealing no character, and presents the influential analysis: by resisting assimilation to the story's design, the pointless detail declares the world's existence beyond the tale's requirements, certifying the whole, meaning sacrificed to produce the signature of the real, realism strategically performing reality's indifference to storytelling.
explanations.D: This choice reads a functional analysis as an editing recommendation. The paragraph explains what the useless detail accomplishes, which is a reason for its presence, not a case for deletion; no prescriptive claim about composition appears.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the anti-realist counter-tradition described in the fifth paragraph is best characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wholly persuaded, since showing the seams is the only honest mode of fiction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: hostile, treating the polemic as an attack on literature itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: indifferent, since the counter-tradition receives only passing mention.

choices.label: D
choices.text: crediting but relativizing, granting that the polemic lands a genuine blow while observing that its devices of exposure were themselves domesticated into convention.

explanations.A: This choice adopts the counter-tradition's own vocabulary of honesty as the author's verdict. The passage grants the polemic force and then narrates its familiar fate, the seams becoming a style, which is incompatible with treating visible seams as the uniquely honest mode; habit domesticates that form like any other.
explanations.B: This choice invents hostility where the passage shows respect. The polemic lands a genuine blow is an acknowledgment of critical success, and the ideology-of-coherence charge is presented with its moral force intact before the domestication point is made.
explanations.C: This choice miscounts the counter-tradition's weight in the argument. It occupies a full paragraph, supplies the second round in the passage's dialectic, and its fate furnishes the principle, no form so disruptive that habit cannot domesticate it, on which the treaty conception partly rests.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph concedes the critique's substance, realism's smoothness as an ideology of coherence, says the polemic lands a genuine blow, and then relativizes it historically: interruptions become expected, readers acclimate, and the devices of exposure settle into a repertoire as conventional as the concealments they indicted, folding both traditions into the treaty account.

questions.stem: The description of plausibility as a treaty, perpetually renegotiated, in the sixth paragraph most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: publishers and authors sign formal agreements governing acceptable content.

choices.label: B
choices.text: what reads as lifelike is a socially maintained, historically shifting agreement between writers and readers about which artifices currently count as none.

choices.label: C
choices.text: readers individually decide, according to private taste, which novels they find believable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: realist novels must be revised in later editions to remain accurate as the world changes.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the metaphor into contract law. The treaty names an implicit cultural understanding about technique and credibility, not documents between commercial parties; no formal agreement of any kind is described.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph states that the reader's sense of how life goes is not private but a socially maintained expectation, and defines plausibility as a treaty, perpetually renegotiated, between writers and readers about which artifices currently count as none, which is why realism has a history and varies across audiences, and why the final paragraph predicts the current invisible devices will become visible.
explanations.C: This choice privatizes what the passage insists is social. The sense of how life goes is explicitly not private; a treaty requires shared expectations across a readership, which is what gives plausibility its history and its variation between audiences rather than between individuals.
explanations.D: This choice returns plausibility to factual accuracy, the correspondence account already retired. The renegotiation concerns which techniques pass unnoticed, a matter of convention, not the updating of a novel's contents to track changes in the world.

questions.stem: Filmgoers in an early era reportedly found certain silent-film acting profoundly lifelike, while modern audiences watching the same performances see exaggerated, stylized gesturing. Based on the third paragraph, this shift is best explained as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: evidence that early audiences lacked the intelligence to detect artifice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: proof that acting techniques have objectively improved since the silent era.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the historical proof-pattern the passage describes, in which techniques that struck one generation as transparency itself strike later generations as visible mannerism, showing the techniques were styles rather than windows.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an anomaly, since the passage holds that conventions, once established, do not change.

explanations.A: This choice converts a historical shift into an insult. The passage's account requires no deficiency in earlier audiences: they perceived through their era's treaty exactly as modern audiences perceive through theirs, and the final paragraph applies the same fate to the present reviewer, who is expressly not deceived.
explanations.B: This choice imposes an objective ranking the convention account excludes. If realism were a window that acting now approximates better, the passage's proof-pattern would fail; the argument is precisely that each era's transparency is a style awaiting visibility, not a rung on a ladder toward accuracy.
explanations.C: Correct. The third paragraph offers this exact structure as its historical proof: techniques that struck one generation as transparency itself strike later generations as mannerism, visible, dated, quaint, which could not happen if the techniques were windows rather than styles. The silent-film case instantiates the pattern in another medium, early lifelikeness becoming legible stylization.
explanations.D: This choice attributes to the passage the opposite of its thesis. Conventions changing over time is the engine of the whole argument, realism has a history, the treaty is perpetually renegotiated, so the acting shift is the expected case, not an anomaly.

questions.stem: Which of the following situations is most analogous to the useless detail's function as described in the fourth paragraph?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A magician plants an assistant in the audience whose apparent spontaneity convinces spectators the volunteers are genuine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A museum removes items from an exhibit that visitors find confusing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A composer deletes a passage that distracts from a symphony's principal theme.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A theme park's designers scatter scuffed, unmarked doors and irrelevant clutter through a recreated street precisely because their pointlessness makes the street feel like a real place rather than a set built only for visitors.

explanations.A: This choice involves manufactured spontaneity that serves the performance's design directly, the plant exists to advance the trick. The useless detail's logic is the reverse: it certifies the world by resisting assimilation to the design, seeming to serve nothing.
explanations.B: This choice removes what does not fit, streamlining toward the exhibit's purpose, which is the tidying impulse the useless detail defies. The device works by inclusion of the unassimilated, not by curatorial subtraction.
explanations.C: This choice subordinates every element to the work's design, deleting whatever fails the theme, exactly the economy whose suspension gives the pointless detail its force. A composition purged of the functionless cannot perform indifference to its own purposes.
explanations.D: Correct. The scuffed doors and irrelevant clutter advance no visitor experience and mark no attraction; their pointlessness declares that the street exists beyond the visit's requirements, certifying the whole, precisely the fourth paragraph's logic in which meaning is sacrificed to produce the signature of the real and the design performs reality's indifference to its purposes.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the third paragraph's claim that realism is the set of artifices a given audience has stopped noticing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that creative writing programs teach the realist repertoire explicitly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cross-era and cross-cultural studies finding that a stable core of narrative techniques is judged lifelike by all audiences regardless of their prior exposure or period, with those judgments never converting to perceptions of mannerism over centuries of reception.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some readers prefer fantasy and never read realist fiction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that reviewers frequently disagree about which contemporary novels deserve praise.

explanations.A: This choice shows the repertoire is teachable and named among practitioners, which the convention account happily accommodates: writers can know the devices while audiences do not register them, and the claim concerns what audiences notice, not what workshops codify.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim entails that lifelikeness is relative to an audience's habituation, so techniques should read as transparent only where familiar and should convert to visible mannerism as treaties shift. A stable core judged lifelike across eras and cultures, independent of exposure, and never converting to mannerism over centuries, would indicate the techniques function as windows after all, breaking the equation of realism with unnoticed artifice.
explanations.C: This choice describes readers who opt out of the genre, which leaves the claim untouched: the convention account concerns how realist effects work on the audiences that encounter them, not the universality of the genre's appeal.
explanations.D: This choice reports disagreement about literary merit, a matter of evaluation, while the claim concerns the mechanism of the reality effect; reviewers could dispute quality endlessly while all perceiving the same techniques as transparent.
